artifacts.content:
Subject: Catching you up on… everything

Hey Mike,

Kids are finally in bed, Emily’s grading on the couch, and I’m still smelling faintly like smoke even though the fire was a month ago. I realized I never really walked you through what happened with the latest kitchen fiasco and the work stuff.

The grease fire on February 27, 2025 was honestly the perfect storm of my own stupidity and stress. I had a pan of oil heating on high on the gas range, planning to throw in some veggies before Liv’s reading homework. Mark Ellison pinged me on Teams about the performance improvement plan he put me on earlier that week, and I stepped into the dining room with my laptop so the kids wouldn’t hear. I got sucked into defending timelines and “leadership presence,” and by the time I smelled something, the oil had flashed and scorched the backsplash and the underside of the upper cabinets.

It wasn’t as bad as the January 2023 fire, but it was enough to set off the ADT smoke detectors on both floors and freak Olivia out again. I grabbed the extinguisher from the pantry like we practiced after the 2023 loss, killed the gas, and got it out before the flames climbed. Hingham Fire came anyway just to be safe. No injuries, just more smoke cleanup and my ego in ashes.

All of this is layered on top of the PIP and the anxiety stuff. You remember I did that three‑day voluntary inpatient stay at McLean back in January for the panic attacks; this was my first real “test” week back trying to juggle work and normal life. Not exactly a confidence builder.

Anyway, we’re okay, the house is okay, and I’m back to treating oil like nitroglycerin. Call when you get a breather.

– J

Title: Thursday Evening Chaos – Ben OT + Liv Reading + Dinner

Date: 2025-03-18
Time: 5:15 p.m. – 8:00 p.m.
Location: Home / Norwell / Zoom
Attendees: Jonathan, Emily, Olivia, Ben

Notes:

5:15 p.m. – Emily and Ben leave for South Shore Children’s Therapy in Norwell (OT session 5:45–6:45). Pack Ben’s fidget toy and snack.

Meanwhile I’m on solo duty at 184 Willow Brook Lane: start dinner early so it’s ready before Liv’s 7:00 p.m. virtual reading support session through East Elementary’s special services program. Remember: NO walking away from the stove with oil on the burner. Last time, on March 18, 2025 around 6:45 p.m., I tried to multitask and we ended up with another small kitchen grease fire while I was rushing to get food ready before her Zoom. Flames stayed on the range but still smoked up the cabinets and set off the ADT alarm. Do not repeat that performance.

6:00–6:40 p.m. – Prep simple, low‑oil dinner (pasta, not stir‑fry). Keep extinguisher and metal lid on counter. Crack kitchen window so the alarm doesn’t lose its mind over steam.

6:45 p.m. – Quick cleanup, get Liv changed and logged into her reading support session on the Chromebook at the dining room table. Headphones charged.

7:00–7:30 p.m. – Liv’s reading support. I stay nearby but off‑camera in case she needs tech help. Emily and Ben back around 7:10–7:15; remind Ben not to barrel through the kitchen mid‑session.

8:00 p.m. – Kids’ showers, bedtime reading. Double‑check stove knobs before going upstairs.

metadata.title: Thursday Evening Chaos – Ben OT + Liv Reading + Dinner
metadata.location: Home / South Shore Children’s Therapy, Norwell / Zoom

artifacts.content:
Title: Therapy prep – fire, McLean, and work stuff

Date: 2025-01-15

Things to bring up with Dr. K at South Shore Behavioral Health:

1) McLean stay + coming home
– Three‑day voluntary inpatient at McLean Hospital in Belmont for severe anxiety and panic attacks earlier this month. Weird mix of relief and shame about going.
– First night trying to “be normal” again was January 9, 2025. Wanted to prove to Emily and the kids (and myself) that I could just cook dinner and handle a regular evening.
– Oil started smoking on the gas range and I completely overreacted. Instead of calmly sliding the pan off the burner, I panicked, grabbed it wrong, and basically sloshed hot grease toward the flame. It splashed onto the burner and ignited a small grease fire. ADT alarm blaring, kids crying, me back in that tunnel‑vision panic.
– Need to talk about how quickly I go from “fine” to catastrophe brain, especially around the kitchen after the 2023 fire.

2) Work / Harborline
– Still rattled from being on the 60‑day performance improvement plan Elena started last year and now Mark Ellison breathing down my neck about deadlines. Feels like any mistake (even burning dinner) proves their point that I’m not “management material.”

3) Home routines
– Basement treadmill helps a bit; consider scheduling runs right after work calls.
– Maybe we need a hard rule: I don’t take Teams or phone calls while I’m cooking with oil, period.

4) Family
– How much to tell Olivia about McLean vs. “Dad needed a reset weekend.”

metadata.title: Therapy prep – fire, McLean, and work stuff

artifacts.content:
Subject: Mom’s bill + that stupid fire

Hi Sarah,

I’m hijacking your professional brain for a second because I’m still steamed about Mass General Brigham and also embarrassed about how it literally turned into a fire.

On April 4, 2025 I logged into the Mass General Brigham patient portal to look at Mom’s recent cardiac workup from South Shore Hospital and saw that $3,200 bill sitting there like a bad joke. I called their billing office from the dining room so I could actually hear over the kids, got put on hold, and started pacing.

In the middle of all that, I completely forgot I’d left a pan of oil heating on high on the gas range. By the time the hold music cut out and a human finally picked up, the smoke alarms were screaming. I dropped the phone, ran back into the kitchen, and sure enough the oil had caught and was licking up the backsplash. Déjà vu from 2023.

Thankfully, the ADT system worked, I grabbed the extinguisher, and it stayed a “small kitchen grease fire” instead of something that took out half the first floor. Still, the kids were shaken and Emily is (fairly) furious that I keep mixing stressful phone calls with cooking.

I ended up calling back later from the upstairs office once everyone had calmed down. The billing rep kept insisting “it’s coded correctly,” but I’m not convinced. From your pediatric NP perspective, does $3,200 for a pretty straightforward cardiac workup at South Shore sound even remotely reasonable for a Medicare patient? I’m wondering if there’s some magic phrase I should use when I call again.

Anyway, Mom’s okay, house is okay, my pride is not. Hope your week on the floor hasn’t been as chaotic as ours.

– Jon

Subject: Pat, court, and the fire

Hey Rach,

I’ve been meaning to write since everything blew up in November and then literally almost blew up in our kitchen.

The night before the mediation at Norfolk Probate and Family Court about your mom possibly moving in with us – November 22, 2024 – Em and I were in the kitchen at 184 Willow Brook going in circles about caregiving schedules. We’d just gotten the mild cognitive impairment diagnosis from Beth Israel Deaconess–Milton, and the idea of Pat living here even temporarily had everyone on edge.

We were arguing about who could cover which days, how it would affect your trips up from Providence, whether Dad and Linda could realistically help, etc. I had a pan of oil heating on the stove, planning to throw together a quick stir‑fry between homework and printing out directions for court. In the middle of one of those “that’s not what I said” loops, I completely forgot about the burner.

Next thing I knew, Liv was yelling that something smelled weird, and the oil had started to smoke and then flare. Small grease fire, but it was enough to scorch the underside of a cabinet and send the ADT alarms into full meltdown mode. Kids were terrified, Emily was shaking, and I was standing there thinking, “This is exactly what we promised ourselves wouldn’t happen again after January 2023.”

We got it out quickly – extinguisher, lid, windows open – and Hingham Fire checked everything. No one hurt, just more smoke cleanup and one more reminder that we can’t have heavy conversations hovering over a gas range.

I know you’re carrying a lot with Pat’s diagnosis too. When you’re up next, maybe we can sit at the dining room table (far away from the stove) and map out something that doesn’t burn all of us out.

Love,
Jon

Subject: Dad, the June fire, and the wall

Em,

Putting this in writing so we don’t lose track of what the contractor said (and so I stop replaying it in my head at 3 a.m.).

When South Shore Restoration came by last week to look at the back wall behind the range – the section Dad singed during that small kitchen fire while babysitting in June 2023 – he confirmed what we already knew from the inspection: standard wood‑frame construction with vinyl siding over plywood sheathing and a poured concrete foundation at 184 Willow Brook.

He pulled off a couple of pieces of drywall behind the stove and showed me the studs. There was some minor smoke charring on a few of them, but he said structurally they’re fine. His exact words were something like, “You could open a claim, but given your $1,000 deductible, I’d probably just prime, repaint, and maybe sister one stud if it makes you sleep better.” So basically: cosmetic, not a repeat of January 2023.

Dad was mortified, obviously. He kept apologizing and talking about that fatal kitchen fire he worked on in Weymouth back in ’96. I tried to remind him we’ve all now had our own little grease‑fire episodes and that the ADT system plus the extinguishers did exactly what they’re supposed to do.

I took some pictures of the open wall and the contractor’s notes; I’ll drop them in our shared “House Projects” folder. For now, I think we patch, repaint, and maybe use this as the final nudge to replace that backsplash you’ve always hated.

Also: can we make a house rule that no one over 70 cooks here without one of us hovering? Dad agreed to that one, half‑joking, but I think he meant it.

– J

metadata.subject: Dad, the June fire, and the wall

artifacts.content:
Title: House systems + health & work overlap

Date: 2024-06-10

Just trying to keep a single place where the house, health, and job stuff intersect so I don’t lose the thread.

Construction / systems
– 184 Willow Brook is wood frame with vinyl siding over plywood and a poured concrete foundation. Roof is architectural asphalt shingles with ice and water shield along eaves/valleys (replaced in 2017 after that inspection flagged aging shingles; still glad we did it before any ice dam claims).
– Plumbing: mostly original copper supply lines with PVC drains, but during the 2019 kitchen/master bath remodel we added a PEX manifold system. I pushed for that after watching our old neighbor Mark Dempsey’s catastrophic copper burst in Feb 2018 and Hanover partially denying his claim.
– Wiring: all copper with a 200‑amp panel; no aluminum or knob‑and‑tube. Dad and the electrician confirmed this in 2017. Still grateful after hearing Dad’s stories about that fatal Weymouth fire.
– Heating: forced hot air natural gas furnace with central AC, programmable thermostats on both floors. We keep it no lower than 62°F in winter, even when we travel.

Safety / losses
– January 2023 grease fire claim (~$7,800) was the wake‑up call: ADT system, extinguishers in kitchen/garage/basement, automatic water shutoff with leak sensors under sink, behind fridge, near water heater.
– Since then: several “near misses” – Dad’s June 2023 babysitting fire, my January 9, 2025 post‑McLean panic‑cooking flare‑up, and the February 27, 2025 PIP/Teams call fire with Mark Ellison. Pattern = stress + distraction + stove.

Work / mental health
– Burnout + panic attacks → three‑day voluntary inpatient at McLean (Jan 2025) and ongoing therapy at South Shore Behavioral.
– Elena’s 60‑day PIP at Harborline and now Mark riding me; FMLA lets me work from the second‑floor home office 4 days/week.

Action items
– Hard rule: no calls while cooking with oil.
– Keep practicing fire drill with kids.
– Maybe move more weeknight meals to sheet‑pan/oven.
– Talk to Dr. K about why my brain goes straight to catastrophe around the stove.

From: Jonathan Carter <jonathan.carter82@gmail.com>
To: Lisa Donnelly <ldonnelly@donnelly-reardonlaw.com>
Date: May 18, 2021 9:42 PM
Subject: Question re: inspection notes for 184 Willow Brook Lane

Hi Lisa,

I’ve been going back through the Harbor Home Inspections report from May 12, 2021 for 184 Willow Brook Lane, Hingham, MA and wanted to run something by you before we finalize everything.

The inspector from Harbor Home Inspections in Braintree, MA described the place as older wood-frame construction with vinyl siding, a poured concrete foundation, and an asphalt shingle roof. That all sounded pretty standard, but the section on electrical raised a flag for me.

He noted that some of the original knob-and-tube wiring is still present in a few interior walls. What complicates it is that my father, Robert (“Bob”) Carter – my dad and a retired electrician – did a partial DIY replacement of that knob-and-tube back in the late 1980s when money was tight for the prior owners (he knew them through a friend). The inspector specifically warned that, because the work wasn’t fully brought up to current code and not all the old wiring was removed, this could be an issue for some insurers if they knew the full history.

Two questions:
1) Is this something we’re obligated to affirmatively disclose anywhere beyond providing the inspection report?
2) From your experience, do carriers actually dig into this level of detail, or is it more of a “don’t volunteer extra” situation as long as we’re not misrepresenting anything on the application?

Want to be sure we’re handling this correctly now so it doesn’t bite us later if we switch homeowners policies or refinance.

Thanks,
Jonathan

metadata.subject: Question re: inspection notes for 184 Willow Brook Lane

artifacts.content:
From: Jonathan Carter <jonathan.carter82@gmail.com>
To: Mark Sullivan <mark.sullivan@harborshoreinsurance.com>
Date: March 6, 2023 8:11 AM
Subject: Follow-up on prior mold remediation claim & foundation work

Hi Mark,

Following up on our conversation about the mold remediation claim we filed with our previous homeowners carrier in early 2023, I wanted to summarize the history at 184 Willow Brook Lane, Hingham, MA so you have it in writing.

The house is standard wood-frame construction with vinyl siding over plywood sheathing and a poured concrete foundation. During a nor’easter in October 2022, we noticed minor seepage along one section of the basement wall. It wasn’t a full-on flood, but enough moisture to dampen the base of the drywall and a few cardboard boxes.

We called Coastal Concrete Repair in Hanover, MA. Their tech came out in late October 2022 and injected the visible crack in the poured concrete foundation wall. While he was here, he commented that the original builder had clearly cut corners on the footing drains along that side of the house, which he said explained why we got that localized seepage during the storm.

Because we were worried about hidden moisture and mold, we brought in a remediation company shortly afterward. That work and the associated testing were what triggered the mold remediation insurance claim with our prior carrier earlier this year. To my knowledge, everything was handled properly and we haven’t had any issues since the crack injection.

Please let me know if your underwriters need any additional documentation from Coastal Concrete Repair or the remediation contractor to fully understand the situation and how it was addressed.

Best,
Jonathan

metadata.subject: Follow-up on prior mold remediation claim & foundation work

artifacts.content:
Title: House systems / insurance notes
Date: April 2, 2023
Author: Jonathan Carter

– Address: 184 Willow Brook Lane, Hingham, MA. Standard wood-frame construction, vinyl siding, poured concrete foundation, asphalt shingle roof.

– Electrical: Need to keep in mind what came up in the Harbor Home Inspections report dated May 12, 2021. Inspector from Harbor Home Inspections (Braintree, MA) flagged that some original knob-and-tube wiring is still present in a few interior walls. Dad (Robert Carter – father and retired electrician) did a partial DIY replacement of that knob-and-tube in the late 1980s, but not everything was brought fully up to current code. Inspector warned this could be a problem for certain insurers if they know the full story.

– Basement / water: After the nor’easter in October 2022, we had minor seepage along one wall in the basement. Coastal Concrete Repair (Hanover, MA) came out and injected the crack in the poured concrete foundation. Their tech said the original builder cut corners on footing drains on that side of the house, which is likely why we had the moisture issue. That seepage + follow-on cleanup is what led to the mold remediation claim with our previous homeowners carrier in early 2023.

– To-do before next renewal / any refinance:
   • Ask licensed electrician to document condition of remaining knob-and-tube and any upgrades.
   • Keep Coastal Concrete Repair invoice + photos handy in case a new insurer asks for details.
   • Double-check how application questions about prior water / mold claims are worded.
